abstract: This research note reports a field study evaluating whether psychological inoculation ('prebunking') against emotional manipulation can be scaled on Instagram. The authors ran a 19-second prebunking video as a Story Feed ad to 375,597 UK Instagram users aged 18–34 and used the platform's poll sticker functionality to assess whether treated users could better identify fearmongering in a fictitious headline. Treated users were 21 percentage points more accurate than controls at identifying emotional manipulation, the effect persisted at a five-month follow-up, and treated users clicked through to learn more at roughly three times the control rate.
key_claims: Brief inoculation videos can be deployed and scaled cheaply as ads in real-world social media scroll feeds, not just in lab settings.
A short prebunking ad substantially improves users' ability to recognize emotional manipulation (fearmongering) in news headlines.
Inoculation effects against a single, focused manipulation technique can persist for at least five months at the group level.
Prebunking exposure increases information-seeking behavior, as evidenced by higher click-through rates to additional content.
Quasi-experimental designs leveraging existing ad platform functionality (poll stickers, birth-month targeting) offer a viable methodology for field-testing misinformation interventions on Instagram.
methods: Field experiment on Instagram in partnership with Google Jigsaw and Reality Team, targeting UK users aged 18–34.
Designed a 19-second prebunking video using a weakened-dose fearmongering example ('Yoga linked to terrifying full body cancer') and ran it as a Story Feed ad from February 5–11, 2025.
Quasi-experimental assignment: control group selected by birth month (April, July, October), since true randomization is not possible in the Instagram ad manager.
Inclusion criterion ('as-treated' design): treatment users had to watch at least 50% of the video to enter the watchlist.
Outcome measured via Instagram Poll Sticker with a single binary question asking users to identify the manipulation technique in a headline; correct answer placed left to require intentional swiping.
Initial poll responses (n = 806; 403 per group) collected 24 hours to 10 days after ad exposure; follow-up poll administered five months later (July 2025; n = 432 control, n = 244 treatment).
Pilot A/B tests of video variants (color, tone, length, music) to optimize hook and hold rates before the main campaign.
Statistical analysis using two-proportion z-tests / chi-square tests with effect sizes (Cohen's h) and 95% confidence intervals; click-through rates computed as clicks/impressions.
findings: Treatment group correctly identified emotional manipulation 59.55% of the time vs. 38.21% in controls, a 21.4 percentage point lift (X²(1) = 35.87, p < .001, h = 0.43).
Baseline recognition among controls (38%) was below chance (50%), indicating poor default ability to spot fearmongering.
At five-month follow-up, treatment accuracy was 66.39% vs. 43.98% control—a stable 22.21 percentage point lift (X²(1) = 31.38, p < .001, h = 0.45).
Click-through rate to 'learn more' was approximately three times higher in the treatment group (0.31% vs. 0.11%; X²(1) = 84.61, p < .001), and remained higher at follow-up (0.274% vs. 0.101%).
Treatment-to-watchlist conversion rate was 12.05%; poll response rate was about 1% (0.85% treatment, 0.34% control).
Campaign cost-per-thousand impressions was $8.25, below Meta averages, demonstrating low-cost scalability.
contributions: Provides one of the first real-world field demonstrations of inoculation efficacy in an Instagram scroll-feed environment, extending prior YouTube-based work (Roozenbeek et al., 2022).
Introduces an innovative quasi-experimental methodology using Instagram's poll sticker and birth-month targeting to evaluate interventions where true randomization is unavailable.
Documents unusually long-lasting (five-month) inoculation effects for a single-technique prebunking message, contributing to the literature on inoculation decay.
Demonstrates that prebunking can shift digital behavior (CTR), not just recognition, suggesting downstream behavioral relevance.
Offers practical guidance and cost benchmarks for policymakers, NGOs, and platforms seeking to scale prebunking campaigns.
framing: The paper situates itself within inoculation theory and the broader misinformation/prebunking literature, addressing the ecological validity gap between lab-based inoculation studies and real-world social media use. It positions emotional manipulation (the appeal-to-emotion fallacy) as a high-impact form of misleading content and frames the study as a scalable, applied complement to earlier YouTube field trials and meta-analytic evidence on inoculation efficacy.
